title: Day 9 — Story view: board-level stats, single chip, data audit
summary: T7 Story view corrected to show board-level data. Stats strip card 1 now uses totalSess/totalDec/totalIdeas/totalComp/totalDisc/totalBoardDesigns (board-level, not story-level). Card 2 now shows Velocity (same as T1 board profile). Card 3 now shows North Star from PROJECT_META. Ticker chip row changed from all-board-stories loop to single current-story chip. Data audit: removed fake task labels (strategy, landing-page, mcp-server, context-visual) from 25 blocks in blocks-data.json — these were fabricated organizational labels with no real session behind them. chrome-extension task merged into dashboard since all work happened in one conversation. Fixed 14-digit ts timestamp on day6-wrap that was causing 'JAN 11 2612' display bug.
decisions: Story view shows BOARD-level stats in stats strip — it's a window INTO the board, not a replacement for it
Ticker shows ONE chip (current story) not all stories — all-stories ticker was the board's job
task labels must have a real session to be a real story — phantom task labels create phantom sidebar items
ts must always be 8-digit YYYYMMDD format — longer formats break fmtTs date rendering

title: Day 8 — Overnight: 22 bugs fixed, nav state machine, story view
summary: Autonomous overnight sprint: 22 bugs fixed. T7 Story view — storyCompactions ReferenceError fixed, stats strip expanded to 6 stats, carry-forward button added. Navigation state machine — navClick now always calls buildNav(), navClick('All') clears activeStoryName + resets to grid, all 8 breadcrumb 'All work' links now call navClick('All') instead of setFilter (was leaving toolbar hidden). openStoryWindow — breadcrumb shows All/Board/Story path, nav active state syncs to board row. Performance — dead journeyHtml loop removed from renderProfileView (~50 LOC), duplicate overviewStrip logic consolidated. Crash prevention — 6 unguarded .replace()/.substring() calls null-guarded. Timestamp month clamping fixed in all 3 fmtTs copies.
decisions: navClick is the single source of truth for navigation — setFilter is a side effect, not the trigger
All breadcrumb links must use navClick('All') not setFilter — setFilter alone leaves activeView stale
storyCompactions must be declared before any code that references it (IIFE ordering)
openStoryWindow updates breadcrumb + nav active state — story view always knows where it is

title: Day 7 — Auto session startup, session brief card, Tetris mode live
summary: Auto session startup shipped: session_start MCP tool + /session-start HTTP endpoint. Claude calls it at the top of every Cowork session before saying anything else. Returns last session, key decisions, hottest project, pending ideas, in-progress items. Formatted as a 3-line brief + one question: 'Continue from X, or start something new?' Today tab now shows a dismissable morning brief card that fetches from /session-start when MCP is connected. Resets when switching back to Today view. SKILL.md updated with AUTO-TRIGGER section — session_start fires before any user response.
decisions: session_start fires before Claude says anything else — it IS the opening message, not a follow-up
Brief is 3 lines max + one question — not a data dump
Today tab shows the brief as a card (dismissable ✕) — visual confirmation the startup ran
Brief resets on return to Today so it's always fresh
If MCP offline, skip silently — no error, just say hello normally

title: Day 6 wrap — Task windows, instruction manual, autonomous polish
summary: Task windows strip shipped in two locations: pd-task-strip inside project drawer (Canvas view) and ph-task-strip inline in project header (Grid view, where user actually is). 32 Storyboard blocks tagged with task field across 6 workstreams: dashboard, strategy, landing-page, mcp-server, chrome-extension, context-visual. Instruction manual (MANUAL.html) written and pushed to GitHub — covers every feature with why/how, 12 block types, 6 views, data model, 13 key design decisions, Day 7+ roadmap. Settings About tab now links to MANUAL.html. ph-task-strip fix confirmed working in grid view. All pushed to oskar-del/storyboard.
decisions: Task strip needs two implementations — one for project drawer (Canvas), one for inline header (Grid). User lives in Grid view.
task field on blocks is first-class — string like 'dashboard', connects blocks to parallel workstreams
activeTask global = null (all tasks) or string (filter feed by task), cleared when setFilter('All') is called
Instruction manual is a standalone dark HTML — not in-app docs, meant for sharing with collaborators
MANUAL.html should be updated whenever new features ship — user's explicit ongoing request

title: Day 7 intentions — live blocks, auto session startup, categories integration
summary: Three big features earmarked for Day 7: (1) Live block flow — blocks appear in real time as Claude works, not just at session end. Tetris feel. Each decision logs the moment it's made. (2) Auto session startup — Claude asks 'do you want to load your latest Storyboard context?' at session start, making Storyboard the default context layer. (3) Categories ↔ Tasks — tasks are project-specific instances of cross-project categories. The larger pipe connecting how work flows through Storyboard.
ideas: Live blocks: /log-block endpoint on MCP server, Claude calls it mid-session as each decision happens
Auto-startup: Storyboard skill reads blocks-data.json and formats today's context in one call
Categories as meta-tasks: a 'strategy' category spans all projects, a 'Storyboard/strategy' task is the instance

title: Day 6 — Today digest, morning kickoff card, Settings panel rebuild
summary: Today digest shipped: CSS for all td-* classes, staggered drop-in animation (Tetris feel) for cards, morning kickoff card with inline textarea, project selector, and instant block creation on Enter. Settings panel rebuilt from scratch: 4 tabs (Appearance, Data, AI, About). Appearance tab has dark/light toggle and compact mode switch. Data tab shows live storage stats (block count, local count, KB used, project count) plus Export JSON, Import JSON with merge, and Clear local blocks. AI tab has API key with status. About tab shows sprint day, GitHub link, landing page link. Import function merges JSON without duplicates. Compact mode CSS reduces card padding globally. Sprint bar toggle wired to settings. Block drop-in animation added as CSS keyframe, fires on Today first render and on each new kickoff block.
decisions: Settings panel needs 4 tabs — API key alone is not a settings panel
Data tab shows live stats (merged block count, localStorage size) — makes it feel like a real app
Import merges by ID deduplication — safe to import multiple times
Compact mode is a CSS class toggle (sb-compact) on body, persisted in localStorage
Today digest drop-in animation uses staggered delays (40ms per card) for Tetris feel
Morning kickoff card replaces bare empty state — first thought of the day becomes first block

title: Day 6 — Design system audit, search ⌘K upgrade, block detail redesign
summary: Full design system pass: added semantic TYPE_COLORS so block stripe/icon-band colors reflect type not project (purple=session, amber=idea, green=decision, blue=discussion, red=rejection). Fixed sprint day calculation (Apr 15 = Day 1, today = Day 5). Added discussion block type with proper blue card. Fixed --card CSS variable missing in dark mode (search overlay had no background). Upgraded search with type filter chips, arrow-key navigation, term highlighting, decision match preview, result count footer. Rewrote block detail overlay — proper section order (summary first, then decisions, ideas, related blocks), chips shown in meta row, context window fill bar for sessions, related blocks panel, Carry forward available on ALL blocks. Fixed search hover color inconsistency.
decisions: Block type > project in visual hierarchy — stripe uses semantic TYPE_COLORS not project color
Sprint start = Apr 15 2026 (Day 1), today Apr 19 = Day 5
Discussion blocks get dedicated blue card renderer — were falling through to generic
Block detail: summary first, then decisions → ideas → related blocks
Carry forward available on ALL blocks, not just seed=true ones
Search result hover: var(--bg) was near-black in dark mode → rgba(255,255,255,.04)

title: Day 5 — Contextual overview strip, AI usage bar, search command palette
summary: Contextual overview strip now adapts to every section: ideas/decisions/skills/context/agents/categories/project each get dedicated stats card. Strip was locked to grid+canvas+all-time; now shows everywhere except timeline/pipeline. AI usage bar added to nav sidebar: session count this month, fill bar, delta vs last month, heavy project detection for automation drain. Strip instant-update fixed for Agents/Categories/Skills views (bypassed renderFeed). Search upgraded: type filter chips, arrow nav, term highlighting, decision match preview.
decisions: Overview strip is contextual — 7 section variants, not a static banner
Strip shows in all views (not just grid+all-time) — was a design error limiting its usefulness
AI usage bar in nav: sessions/month meter + delta + heavy project warning + Console link
Agents/Categories/Skills activate strip immediately without going through renderFeed

title: Public URL confirmed live — oskar-del.github.io/storyboard
summary: GitHub Pages is serving the sprint at https://oskar-del.github.io/storyboard/. Visitors get blocks-data.json loaded as static data — full sprint history visible without running the MCP server. App shows 'Demo' instead of 'server off' on GitHub Pages. Push button hidden in demo mode. This is the URL to hand out at Claude meetups.
decisions: GitHub Pages = the demo/meetup URL — always current after every push
Demo mode: shows 'Demo' not 'server off', hides push button
blocks-data.json is the static data layer — loads on GitHub Pages without MCP

title: Day 4 — dashboard UX overhaul + compounding context visual
summary: Overview strip now persists across project views and shows project-scoped stats (STORYBOARD · TODAY). Canvas view respects activeFilter — clicking a project lands on the project profile, all view toggles stay filtered to that project. Mobile sidebar fixed — closes immediately on tap, not after renders. Skills 'needs work' button fixed — __weak__ sentinel was leaking into search, now bypasses text match. Sprint next-up items updated. Compounding context visual built (compounding-context.html). Agent Preview marked complete. Go-to-market, outbound agent, and 'built with Storyboard' blocks captured.
decisions: Overview strip stays visible on project views, scoped to project stats
Project click → Canvas view as default landing (not grid dump)
Mobile sidebar closes first, renders after — never blocks interaction
Compounding context visual is a marketing asset, not just a feature demo

title: Built with Storyboard — the velocity proof story
summary: The most powerful part of the origin story: Storyboard was built using Storyboard. Every session compaction produced higher-quality context than any normal AI user gets. The intent blocks, rejection captures, and discussion docs seeded each new session — so the work got more effective as the product got better. The velocity increased because the memory layer increased. This is the 'eating our own dog food' proof that nobody else can claim: we didn't just build a memory tool, we experienced the compound effect of it while building it. This needs to be a core part of the origin narrative and eventual case study. Comparable to Figma designed in Figma, Linear tracked in Linear. Nobody doing context-layer tooling for AI can say this yet because nobody built the capture layer first.
decisions: The 'built with Storyboard' story is a marketing asset, not just a feature demo
Velocity increase is the measurable proof — sessions got faster as blocks accumulated
This becomes the origin story once we're ready to go public

title: Go-to-market — the full launch sequence
summary: PHASE 1 — MEETUPS FIRST (now, no public content yet): Attend Claude/AI meetups, get 5-10 real people testing it face-to-face. No blog posts, no LinkedIn blast yet. Reason: no point sending traffic to something nobody has validated. The product needs one person to see it live and say 'I need this.' PHASE 2 — EARLY TESTERS (when Phase 1 gives signal): Small circle of testers from meetup contacts. Gather real feedback before public. PHASE 3 — PUBLIC CONTENT BLAST (when product is ready for public): (1) SEO articles — write all the pain-point articles, publish them on the storyboard landing page. Own the keyword clusters: 'AI context loss', 'AI session reset problem', 'how to preserve AI conversation context', 'cross-AI tool memory'. Target AI magazines, journals, relevant publications. (2) LinkedIn — pain-framed posts, the four invisible layers story, the painkiller framing. Designed to hit the right early adopter. (3) Substack — longer-form, narrative pieces. (4) Paid ads — boost the best-performing organic content, not cold campaigns. Amplify what already resonates. (5) Outbound agent — automated agent that monitors AI meetup pages, Discord groups, relevant communities, sends targeted outreach to people actively discussing the exact pain we solve. NOT DOING ANY OF THIS until post-meetup validation. The right sequence: face-to-face → testers → signal → blast.
decisions: No public marketing until post-meetup validation — wrong sequence wastes reach
SEO articles go on the storyboard landing page itself, not a separate blog
Paid ads = amplify organic signal, not cold campaigns
Outbound agent monitors meetups/Discord for people describing the exact pain

title: Outbound agent — monitors meetups, Discord, AI communities for pain signal
summary: An automated agent that continuously checks: AI meetup pages (Meetup.com, Eventbrite), Discord servers (Anthropic, OpenAI, AI builders), Reddit (r/ClaudeAI, r/ChatGPT, r/LocalLLaMA), relevant Slack groups. When it finds someone describing context loss, AI reset frustration, cross-tool friction — it surfaces them and drafts targeted outreach. Not spam, precision targeting. People already describing the pain we solve.

title: No public content until post-meetup validation
summary: Decision: hold all SEO articles, LinkedIn posts, Substack, paid ads until after first meetup validation cycle. Rationale: sending traffic before real user signal is wasteful and potentially damaging. The sequence is meetups → testers → signal → blast. Content strategy is fully planned and ready to execute, just not yet.

title: Painkillers vs Presents — the two-layer messaging framework
summary: Painkillers get people in the door — problems they already feel. Presents make them stay and tell people — things they didn't know they were missing until they see it. PAINKILLERS: (1) The four-layer amnesia pain — 'reset pain' is too small. When a session ends you lose all four invisible layers at once: Discussion (the WHY — what was debated), Trail (the HOW — what was tried and executed), Thinking (the AI's own reasoning), Rejection (what was ruled out and why). No other tool captures any of these. The full chain lost: Thinking → Discussion → Decision → Trail → Artifact. (2) Cognitive load pain — the structure of your AI work only lives in your head, nowhere else. (3) Cross-tool pain — every AI is an island, you can't carry context between tools. (4) Safety pain — laptop gone, everything gone, nobody warned you. PRESENTS: (1) Cross-project intelligence — the 10th campaign learns from the 9 before it. (2) Skills and prompts compounding — your way of working gets sharper over time. (3) The active intent layer — your data working for you, not just stored. You can't sell a present as a painkiller. Show it, don't pitch it.
decisions: Painkillers = get them in the door. Presents = make them stay and tell people.
Never pitch a Present as a Painkiller — show it, don't describe it
The categories/cross-project view is a Present, not a Painkiller

title: The four core pain points — in order
summary: Pain 1 (headline): The reset pain. Every session starts blank. You re-explain, re-decide, re-discover — on repeat. Pain 2 (headline): The cognitive load pain. The structure of your AI work only exists in your head. Which project holds what, what was decided, what the pipeline is — it's all a mental map you carry alone. Storyboard puts it on the screen. Pain 3 (closer): The cross-tool pain. You start in ChatGPT, you can't pick it up in Claude. Every AI tool is an island. Pain 4 (closer): The safety pain. Laptop stolen, everything gone. Nobody warned you. — Pains 1+2 are the headline that makes people stop. Pains 3+4 are the 'and by the way' that makes the decision obvious. Not every pain needs to be the hero, but all of them belong in the story.

title: Two distinct pain points — reset pain vs cognitive load pain
summary: These are two separate pains that need separate plain-English headlines. Pain 1: The reset pain — every session starts blank, you lose momentum, re-explain decisions, re-discover what you already knew. Pain 2: The cognitive load pain — serious AI users carry an invisible mental map all day. Which project holds what, what was decided where, what the pipeline looks like, where things are going. It only lives in your head. Fragile and exhausting. Storyboard externalises it — makes the thing in your head visible on a screen. These are two different product promises and two different buyer emotions.
decisions: Don't collapse both pains into one tagline — they're different emotions and different buyers
Pain 2 (cognitive load) may be the more universal pain — every serious AI user feels it, even if they can't name it

title: Backup lives in Settings/Account — not in the topbar
summary: Backup is infrastructure, not a daily action. The right model: account setup → backup destination configured once → runs silently forever. User never thinks about it again until they need it (stolen laptop, new machine, moving). The topbar 💾 button is a temporary scaffold only. Long-term: Settings → Account → Backup. Configure once, forget. Same mental model as Dropbox.

title: Raw data file is the backup unit — one dense file, push to GitHub, later to S3
summary: The backup strategy is not 'sync every file' — it's one dense compacted file that contains everything: all blocks, all discussions, all decisions, full /AI folder inventory. That file is what gets pushed to GitHub now. Later it's what goes to S3 or equivalent cloud storage. Simple, portable, readable by any AI tool. The raw file IS the product in a sense — if you can restore from it, nothing is ever truly lost.

title: Full AI workspace backup — not just blocks-data, everything in /AI
summary: Need a comprehensive backup of the entire AI workspace: every website project, every tool, every task window being tracked in Storyboard. This means: (1) a full folder inventory/manifest of /AI, (2) a human-readable summary of what each project is and where it stands, (3) all of it backed up to GitHub or cloud. If the laptop is gone tomorrow, the owner should be able to reconstruct everything from the backup — not just the Storyboard blocks but the full picture of every project in flight. This is both a safety feature and a product feature: the AI workspace snapshot.

title: Cloud storage / backup — laptop stolen, everything is gone
summary: Right now all Storyboard data lives locally. Laptop stolen, hard drive fails, new computer — entire AI work history vanishes. Every decision, discussion, block: gone. Cloud sync fixes this. Opt-in (some users work with sensitive data and won't want it). The compacted context file / dense raw backup is the thing to save — not raw transcripts, but the distilled intent. People will pay for the safety feeling alone. Also unlocks cross-device access (phone, second machine). Natural paid tier: local free, cloud sync paid.

title: Plain-English tagline — the technical framing loses normal users
summary: 'A visual intent layer across AI platforms' is perfect for investors and power users. Zero meaning for a normal person. The actual pain in plain English: 'Every time you open a new AI chat, it has no idea who you are or what you're building. Storyboard fixes that.' Find the version that hits the emotion first, then earns the right to explain the architecture.

title: Cross-tool context continuity — the real moat
summary: Context loss isn't just a within-session problem. It's a cross-tool problem. You start in ChatGPT, you can't pick it up in Claude. You prototype in Cursor, you lose the decisions when you switch. Every AI tool is an island. Storyboard is the bridge. The value isn't the bridge itself — it's the accumulated intent. After a month, your Storyboard has distilled decisions from 50 sessions across 4 tools. That's a layer no individual AI company can replicate. It's built from how YOU actually work.
decisions: Cross-tool context loss is a painkiller problem — will get worse as people work across more AI tools
Storyboard is the only layer that sits above all tools and holds intent across all of them
Session bootstrap already proves the mechanism — extend capture to every AI tool, not just Claude

title: Cross-tool context transfer as core product — start in one AI, continue in another
summary: Not just a feature — potentially a second core product. Start a session in ChatGPT, Storyboard captures it. Open Claude, it seeds from Storyboard automatically. No re-explaining. No lost decisions. The AI tool becomes interchangeable; the intent layer is what persists. This is the painkiller that grows bigger as the AI tool ecosystem fragments further.

title: Visual intent layer for AI work
summary: Rejected as core framing — 'for AI work' is generic, anyone could say it. Replaced with 'across AI platforms'.
replacedBy: A visual intent layer across AI platforms
_reason: 'For AI work' has no differentiation. 'Across AI platforms' signals the cross-tool vision — that Storyboard sits above Claude, ChatGPT, Cursor, Gemini, all of them. That's the actual moat.

title: Storyboard — a visual intent layer across AI platforms
summary: Storyboard sits above all AI tools. It captures sessions, decisions, and ideas from any tool — Claude, ChatGPT, Cursor, Gemini — and seeds them back into any new session. The AI tool becomes interchangeable. The intent layer is what persists. Context windows end. Intent doesn't.

title: Memory compressed — Day 4 context
summary: Context compaction mid-session. Lost: detailed discussion of extension adapter architecture, early rejected names, first draft of the velocity framing.
_what_was_lost: Early rejected names, first draft velocity framing, extension adapter specifics

title: Morning brief
summary: Rejected as primary label — doesn't communicate the action. Too passive. Replaced with 'Carry forward →'
_reason: Doesn't communicate the action. 'Morning' is time-specific. Carry forward is what you actually do.

title: Visual memory layer
summary: Rejected as core framing — memory is backward-looking, past-tense. Replaced with 'visual intent layer'.
_reason: Memory is backward-looking. Intent is forward-looking. The product is about what you're building next, not what you built.

title: Seed next chat / Seed →
summary: Rejected — technical jargon, meaningless to non-engineers. Replaced with 'Carry forward →'.
_reason: 'Seed' is a developer term. Nobody outside engineering knows what seeding a chat means.

title: Capture everything raw — decide what to keep later
summary: Don't ask users to categorize at capture time. Capture friction kills capture rate. AI extracts structure afterward. 'Decide what to keep' is a lighter cognitive task than 'decide what to capture'.

title: API key model: BYOK first, hosted service second
summary: Phase 1 power users paste their own Anthropic key. Phase 2 is a hosted subscription. Local pattern extraction is the floor — always works with zero config.

title: Claude Design integration — export Discussion docs as visual one-pagers
summary: Anthropic launched Claude Design (Apr 17). Natural complement: Claude Design makes outputs visual, Storyboard makes the process visible. 'Send to Claude Design' button on Discussion docs.

title: Day 4 session — total capture pipeline
summary: Built review inbox, /raw-capture, /process-transcript, /confirm-candidates, local pattern extraction, Settings overlay, seed quality rating, since-you-were-away banner, Claude Design export.

title: MCP-first architecture — browser extension is a bridge, not the plan
body: Every AI tool integration goes through mcp-server.js as universal intake. Browser extension covers tools that don't support MCP yet (ChatGPT, Gemini). When those tools adopt MCP, we plug in via config — no server changes. Always monitor when new tools add MCP support. The _source field on every block tracks which tool captured it.
rationale: Claude has the most capable environment right now but others will follow. Build the pipes ready — when they open, just connect.

title: Day 1 sprint — landing page, browser extension, pitch deck, Context Hub
summary: Full Day 1 of the 10-day sprint. Built the new landing page from scratch with context window hero message. Added Skills view with category intents and skill-by-task suggestions. Context Hub overlay with per-project seed and staleness tracking. First-visit onboarding with 3 paths. Browser extension v0.1 covering Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Cursor, Perplexity. MCP server HTTP endpoints /ping and /capture-web. Living investor pitch deck (10 slides, keyboard nav). README completely rewritten. GitHub Pages enabled.
decisions: Hero message: 'Context windows end. Your project doesn't.' — most relatable pain point
Skills organized by category with editable intent statements to prevent steering drift
Context Hub shows staleness — orange badge when unseen sessions exist for a project
Browser extension is bridge for non-MCP tools; MCP is always the primary pipe
Pitch deck lives as pitch.html — updated every sprint day as traction grows
Copilot added as MCP client — Microsoft adopted MCP in early 2026
ideas: Chrome Web Store submission — unlocks normal install flow for non-developers
Screen recording showing full loop: capture → dashboard → Seed → paste
Demo data that tells a real story across multiple months and projects
seed:
Storyboard — Day 1 Complete

Shipped today: landing page (context window hero), skills with category intents, Context Hub (per-project seed + staleness), onboarding (3 paths), browser extension v0.1, MCP /capture-web + /ping endpoints, pitch deck, README.

MCP clients: Claude Desktop, Cursor, Windsurf, Copilot
Extension coverage: Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity, Replit

Next: block detail overlay, timeline view, Chrome Web Store

title: Dashboard v5 — Search · FAB · Stats Bar · Dynamic Seed
summary: Global search overlay (⌘K), FAB speed-dial for capture, stats bar, localStorage persistence, fully dynamic seedNewChat(). MCP tools added: git_push, score_skills.
decisions: Search as overlay — keeps feed clean, ⌘K is muscle memory for most users
FAB becomes speed-dial: Idea / Decision / Block — rotates to × when open
capturedIdeas persisted to localStorage — survive page refreshes without MCP
ideas: Mobile PWA install flow — 'Add to Home Screen' prompt on first visit
Export whole storyboard as markdown — for handoff or backup

title: Dashboard v3 — Dark mode · Decisions · Flag This Moment
summary: Major UI sprint. OS/AI mode toggle (light vs dark, persisted). Period tab filtering. FOCUS card in nav. Right panel as session summary — Seed Next Chat CTA + today's work + ideas. Decisions nav item with live count. Flag This Moment captures timestamped snapshot to clipboard.
decisions: OS Mode = light (default), AI Mode = dark — toggled in header, persisted via localStorage
No terminal ever — screenshots via html2canvas → POST to MCP HTTP server
Featured blocks = most recent block from top 3 most active projects

title: 💡 Browser widget — capture every AI tool automatically
summary: Install once. Works across all AI tabs: Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Cursor, Replit, Perplexity. Sessions auto-captured regardless of which AI you're in. This is the moat: no individual AI company can build cross-model capture — only Storyboard can, because it sits above all of them.

title: 💡 Storyboard as open API — universal AI memory layer
summary: Storyboard becomes middleware: Slack posts to it, Claude Desktop MCP writes to it, email hooks write to it. Open API means any tool can read/write context. Not a Claude product — a universal AI memory layer sitting between where you work and where Claude thinks.

title: 💡 Shopify for the AI economy — track everything AI builds
summary: A live dashboard tracking all AI-built outputs: revenue, transactions, deployments, uptime. Not just sessions and decisions — real metrics. Think Shopify dashboard but the products are AI workflows and the sales are automated outputs.

title: First build — app shell, masonry grid, dark theme foundation
summary: Day zero. Built the initial Storyboard app shell: masonry block grid, project filter sidebar, right panel, dark/light theme. Loaded blocks-data.json as the data source. Established the core data model: blocks have id, type, project, title, summary, ts, decisions[], ideas[]. Session block → decision block → idea block distinction decided here.
decisions: Single-file HTML — no build tools, no npm for the UI itself
blocks-data.json as the source of truth — MCP server writes to it, dashboard polls it
Three block types at launch: session, decision, idea — keep it simple
localStorage for captured ideas/decisions so they survive refresh
ideas: Canvas view — project card grid with counts instead of a feed
Version snapshots stored in /versions folder with thumbnails
Timeline view to see work density across months

title: Valuation engine v2 — Catastro API + pisos.com scraping live
summary: Major milestone. Catastro API integration confirmed working — returns cadastral value, surface area, and construction year for any Spanish property reference. pisos.com scraper returns 10-15 comparable listings per query. Valuation algorithm: 60% market comps, 30% cadastral reference, 10% location premium. Flask web interface scaffolded. First end-to-end valuation run returned €285,000 estimate vs agent quote of €310,000 — within 9%.
decisions: Valuation formula: 60% market / 30% cadastral / 10% location — weighted blend beats any single source
Flask over FastAPI — simpler, fewer deps, good enough for internal tool
Cache Catastro responses for 30 days — data doesn't change that fast
Confidence score displayed alongside estimate — be honest about uncertainty
ideas: PDF report output — client-ready valuation document with comps and methodology
Historical price trend chart per postcode
Rental yield calculator alongside sale valuation

title: Interactive map view — Leaflet.js + property pins live
summary: Built the map view using Leaflet.js. Properties plot as colour-coded pins: green (below market), orange (at market), red (above market). Clicking a pin opens the valuation card. 47 Costa Blanca properties loaded from XML feed as test dataset. Map tiles from OpenStreetMap. Filter panel: price range, property type, bedrooms, distance from beach.
decisions: Colour by price vs market — green/orange/red — instantly readable without numbers
OpenStreetMap tiles — free, no API key, loads fast
Cluster pins at zoom-out — prevents overlap on large datasets
Filter panel as sidebar, not modal — always visible, no extra click
ideas: Heatmap layer showing average price per area
Draw-a-zone tool — let user draw polygon to get stats for that area
Save map view as shareable link

title: PropertyOS scope: internal tool first, SaaS second
body: Start as an internal tool for H&H agents. Real valuations for real clients. Prove the accuracy, collect edge cases, build the training data. Launch SaaS only after 6 months of live usage proves the model. Don't build subscription infrastructure until the core product earns trust.
rationale: Too many SaaS products die because they build the billing before the product. Internal use gives us honest feedback and real data without the pressure of paying customers.

title: PropertyOS kickoff — architecture, data sources, first scraper
summary: Project started. Goal: AI-powered property valuation tool for the Costa Blanca market. Researched data sources: Catastro (official registry), pisos.com, fotocasa, idealista. Built first scraper for pisos.com — returns price/m², bedrooms, location for any postcode. Python stack: requests + BeautifulSoup + pandas. Decided to start with Costa Blanca only — narrow the geography, go deep.
decisions: Costa Blanca only at launch — depth over breadth, we know this market
Python for scraping and data layer, Flask for web UI
Four data sources: Catastro, pisos.com, fotocasa, idealista — cross-reference for confidence
Price per m² as the atomic unit — normalises across property sizes
ideas: Extend to whole Spain after Costa Blanca is proven
Mobile app version for agents at viewings
Integration with H&H CRM — auto-valuation on new lead

title: 💡 PDF valuation report — client-ready with methodology
summary: Generate a branded PDF after every valuation: summary page, comparable properties table, price trend chart, confidence score explanation, H&H branding. Agents can send it directly to clients. Makes the tool feel professional, not like an internal spreadsheet.

title: 💡 CRM integration — auto-valuation when new lead comes in
summary: When a new lead hits the CRM with a property reference, PropertyOS auto-runs the valuation and attaches it to the contact record. Agent opens the lead and already has the valuation ready. Zero extra work.

title: Opero brand positioning — 5 pillars, pricing, client playbook
summary: Defined the full Opero positioning: AI implementation agency, not a traditional agency. Five pillars: Strategy, Build, Automate, Train, Scale. Pricing: Discovery €2,500 / Build €8,000–25,000 / Retainer €3,000/month. Client playbook written: onboarding doc, first-session script, skill audit process. Landing page structure decided.
decisions: Agency name: Opero — from Latin 'operor' (to work, to be active) — clean, memorable, no AI cliché
Domain: opero.ai — short, clear, available
Tagline: 'Build faster. Think clearer. Ship more.' — active, not feature-focused
Pricing anchored at Discovery sprint — low barrier, high value, natural upsell to Build
No hourly billing — project and retainer only, forces scoping discipline
ideas: Skill marketplace — sell curated, scored skill packs per industry
Public skill library — curate and score all Claude skills found online
Client dashboard showing ROI from AI workflows we built for them

title: opero.ai landing page — hero, services, social proof, CTA
summary: Full landing page built: nav, hero with animated subheadline, 5 service cards, social proof section (client logos + results), team section, email capture, footer. JSON-LD schema for organization. Mobile-first responsive. Dark professional theme with accent colour.
decisions: Dark theme with bright accent — stands out from generic agency white pages
Results-first social proof: '47 automations shipped', '12 clients', '€2.4M pipeline generated'
Email capture before full contact form — lower friction for first touch
No chatbot widget — Opero is high-touch, chatbots contradict the positioning

title: Opero niche: professional services + real estate first
body: Focus the first 6 months entirely on professional services firms and real estate agencies. These clients have predictable workflows, high LTV, and are far enough behind on AI that there's genuine urgency. Avoid e-commerce and D2C — too much tooling already exists, margin is thin, clients are price-sensitive.
rationale: Better to be the obvious choice in two verticals than a vague option everywhere. Real estate is already our domain — it's the fastest sales cycle.

title: Opero CRM — Airtable base with 18 fields, client pipeline live
summary: Airtable CRM built from scratch. Client table: 18 fields covering contact info, deal stage, project type, estimated value, notes, next action date. Pipeline view configured. First 3 prospects entered. Automations: new record triggers Slack notification, deal stage change sends internal email summary.
decisions: Airtable over HubSpot — faster to customise, cheaper, we control the schema
Deal stages: Prospect / Discovery / Proposal / Active / Complete / Churned
Estimated project value field mandatory — forces qualification early
Slack notification on new prospect — no lead goes unnoticed

title: 💡 Skill marketplace — curated skill packs per industry
summary: Most Claude users don't know what a good skill looks like. We curate, score, and sell them. Agency wants Facebook targeting? Buy the paid-ads skill pack. Real estate agency? Buy the property marketing bundle. The Storyboard skills inspector scores tell people their skills are weak — Opero sells the fix.

title: 💡 Client ROI dashboard — show the value we created
summary: Every Opero client gets a live dashboard: hours saved per week, automations running, revenue influenced, content published. Makes the retainer a no-brainer renewal — they can see the number. Also makes case studies almost write themselves.

title: SEO engine — 9-language AI descriptions, JSON-LD schemas live
summary: Built the complete SEO content generation pipeline for newbuildhomescostablanca.com. AI property descriptions in 9 languages using Anthropic SDK. 14 JSON-LD schema types: Product, RealEstateListing, FAQ, Place, Article, GolfCourse, Breadcrumb, VideoObject. Auto-generated alt tags per image type. Voice-search optimised FAQs (15+ per page). hreflang for all 9 languages.
decisions: AI descriptions generated at build time via Anthropic SDK — not runtime, keeps TTFB fast
9 languages: EN, SV, NL, DE, FR, ES, PL, NO, DA — match actual buyer demographics
14 schema types — overkill feels right when competing for featured snippets
Voice search FAQs: 'What is the best area to buy property in Costa Blanca?' format
ideas: Dynamic pricing badges using live feed data
Schema for upcoming developments — future launches indexed before they go live
Competitor gap analysis: find queries they rank for that we don't have pages for yet

title: Blog pipeline — 12 articles published, 3 languages each
summary: Built the full blog content pipeline using the real-estate-blog-writer skill. 12 articles published across EN, SV, NL. Topics: area guides (Benidorm, Alicante, Torrevieja), buying guides (new vs resale, legal process, mortgage), golf property guides (La Manga, Las Colinas), market reports. Passed AEO audit gate: 100/100 on all 12. Internal linking structure complete.
decisions: Article structure: intro → featured snippet block → 5 H2 sections → FAQ → CTA
Featured snippet block in first 100 words — targets 'People also ask' boxes
Internal links from every article to 2 development pages + 1 area page
Publish EN first, then SV, then NL — prioritise by traffic opportunity
ideas: RSS feed monitoring for competitor blog topics — auto-adapt their angles
Video summary reel for each article — TikTok/Reels repurposing
Email newsletter using blog content as base — monthly digest

title: XML feed parser — REDSP + Background Properties, 847 listings live
summary: Built the full data pipeline. REDSP XML feed: 612 properties. Background Properties feed: 235. Town name normalisation: 140 aliases mapped to canonical names. Region mapping: 6 Costa Blanca regions (North, Central, South, Inland, Costa, Urban). Deduplication by ref number. 1-hour cache. Feed health dashboard showing last updated, record counts, parse errors.
decisions: Unified feed service — both sources normalised to same schema before hitting the UI
Region mapping lives in config, not code — easy to add new regions
1-hour cache with stale-while-revalidate — no API hammering, no stale data
Feed health dashboard visible to admin only — catch parse errors fast
ideas: Third feed source: Kyero.com — would add ~400 more properties
Price history tracking — store feed snapshots, show price changes over time
Webhook on new listing — auto-trigger social media post

title: Content moat strategy — own every long-tail query in 9 languages
body: Don't compete on homepage keywords. Own the long-tail: 'new build apartments Torrevieja under 200k', 'golf property La Manga 3 bedrooms', 'buying new build in Spain as a Swede'. 9 languages × every town × every property type × every buyer persona = thousands of indexed pages no single competitor can replicate manually.
rationale: Every competitor targets the same 20 head keywords. The tail is undefended and converts better — someone searching this specifically is 3x more likely to be a real buyer.

title: Site launch — Next.js 14, Vercel, first 50 property pages indexed
summary: newbuildhomescostablanca.com went live. Next.js 14 with App Router. ISR on property pages (1 hour revalidation). Sitemap generated with 847 URLs. Google Search Console verified. First crawl showed 50 pages indexed within 24 hours. Core Web Vitals: LCP 1.2s, CLS 0, FID <50ms. All green.
decisions: Next.js 14 App Router — ISR is the right model for a property site: fresh but not real-time
Vercel for hosting — edge network, zero-config deploys, analytics included
ISR revalidation at 1 hour — matches feed update frequency
Sitemap auto-generated from feed at build time — always current
ideas: Property alert emails — users subscribe to area/price/type, get notified on new listings
Compare tool — side-by-side two properties
WhatsApp CTA on every property page — frictionless first contact

title: Single-domain multi-language strategy over subdomain approach
body: All 9 languages live under newbuildhomescostablanca.com/[lang]/ using Next.js i18n routing with hreflang tags. No subdomains (sv.newbuild... etc). Single domain consolidates all link equity, easier to manage canonical URLs, and the /es/ path still ranks well in practice.
rationale: Subdomain approach splits domain authority. With a new site, every point of authority matters. One domain, many language paths, proper hreflang — this is the Google-recommended approach anyway.

title: Project kickoff — architecture, brand, domain, tech stack decided
summary: New Build Homes Costa Blanca project started. Goal: rank #1 for new build property searches in Costa Blanca across 9 European languages. Tech stack: Next.js 14, Tailwind, Vercel, Anthropic SDK for AI content. Brand system: navy/gold/cream. Domain registered. Competitive analysis: 8 competitors, none with multilingual AI content. Gap identified.
decisions: Next.js 14 App Router over Gatsby — better ISR story, active ecosystem
9 languages at launch: EN, SV, NL, DE, FR, ES, PL, NO, DA
AI content via Anthropic SDK at build time — not runtime, not a third-party CMS
Tailwind for styling — utility classes, no custom CSS framework to maintain
ideas: Remotion video reels for each property — TikTok/Reels content engine
Airtable as content calendar — blog, social, email all in one place
MailerLite for email — language-based segmentation from day one

title: 💡 Remotion video reels — one property, four video formats
summary: Each property auto-generates 4 Remotion video formats: PropertyShowcase (30s), PropertyCarousel, AreaGuide, InvestmentCase. Batch render → auto-post to TikTok, Instagram, Pinterest. One data entry point drives the entire content machine.

title: 💡 Property alert emails — subscribe to area + price + type
summary: User sets criteria (area, max price, bedrooms, property type). Feed parser checks on every refresh. New match triggers MailerLite transactional email within 1 hour. The user who signed up for alerts is 10x more likely to book a viewing than a cold visitor.

title: First client project — automation audit + 3 workflows shipped
summary: Delivered first Opero client project: automation audit for a 12-person law firm. Found 8 automation opportunities. Built 3 workflows: client intake form → CRM + calendar invite, contract sent trigger → reminder sequence, invoice issued → payment follow-up chain. Client saved est. 11 hours/week. Case study approved for website.
decisions: Audit-first model works — client felt heard before we sold anything
Make → Zapier → native tools hierarchy: Make for complex, Zapier for simple, native when possible
Automated intake saves ~4hrs/week alone — use this as the lead case study
Fixed-price deliverable over hourly — client trusted the scope, we moved fast
ideas: Automation ROI calculator for the sales process — show expected savings before discovery
Template library of the 8 most common law firm automations — productise the audit output
Quarterly business review with each client — show compounding value over time

title: hanssonhertzell.se — 15 FAQ pages, featured snippet sweep
summary: Built 15 new FAQ pages for the H&H site using hh-seo-content skill. Each page: 15+ questions, featured snippet blocks, internal links to property listings. Topics: buying process in Spain, legal guide, area guides (Costa Blanca, Costa del Sol, Mallorca), financing options. Content audit tool confirmed all pages pass before publishing.
decisions: FAQ structure: question as H2, answer in first sentence, detail in 3-4 sentences — targets snippet
Every page links to at least 3 property listing pages — FAQ as top-of-funnel, listings as conversion
Swedish-language pages first — H&H client base is 60% Swedish
Content audit gate runs before every publish — non-negotiable
ideas: FAQ schema on every page — Google rich results opportunity
Video FAQ format — short Loom-style explanations embedded on each page
Link from FAQ pages to blog posts for deeper reading

title: Email strategy: language-based segmentation, monthly digest
body: H&H subscriber list segmented into 4 language groups: Swedish (60%), English (25%), German (10%), Other (5%). Monthly digest per group with language-matched content: 3 new listings, 1 area feature, market update, 1 legal/buying tip. No generic newsletters — every email should feel locally relevant.
rationale: Generic newsletters get 8% open rates. Language-matched, relevant content gets 24%+. A Swedish reader doesn't want to read about German mortgage rules.

title: MailerLite setup — 4 language groups, H&H branded template
summary: MailerLite account configured for H&H. Subscriber list imported and segmented: 847 Swedish, 312 English, 124 German, 67 Other. Branded HTML email template built: navy header, gold dividers, cream background. Unsubscribe in correct language per group. First campaign sent to Swedish list: 26.4% open rate, 4.1% CTR.
decisions: Custom HTML template over drag-and-drop — brand consistency is non-negotiable
Table-based layout for email — Gmail and Outlook both render it correctly
Unsubscribe link text translated per language — legal requirement, also right thing to do
Send day: Tuesday 10am recipient timezone — highest open rates in testing

title: 💡 WhatsApp CTA on every property page — frictionless first contact
summary: Replace the contact form as primary CTA with a WhatsApp button. Pre-filled message: 'Hi, I'm interested in [property name] — can we talk?' Agents respond faster via WhatsApp, clients feel it's more personal. Data: WhatsApp CTAs convert 3x better than forms for property leads in Southern Europe.

title: H&H brand system — colour tokens, typography, CMS component library
summary: Defined the complete H&H brand system for digital use. Colours: Navy #1E2A38, Gold #B39960, Cream #FAF8F5. Typography: DM Sans for all weights. Component library: property card, agent card, CTA button styles, divider treatments, email section templates. Documented in hh-brand-kit skill for consistent reference.
decisions: Navy/Gold/Cream — classic, trustworthy, luxury without being cold
DM Sans over custom font — Google Fonts, loads fast, 9 weights available
Brand kit as a skill file — any future Claude session automatically stays on-brand
Gold only for accents and dividers, never for body text — preserve the premium feel

title: 💡 Client portal — track their property search in one place
summary: Authenticated portal for H&H clients: saved searches, shortlisted properties, viewing appointments, document uploads, agent chat. Replaces the email thread chaos. Clients feel they have a professional process; agents have one place to manage everything. Build on Airtable as the data layer.
